When black reformats a multi-line method call (e.g., `AsyncProxyTransport.from_url(proxyUrl)` → split across 3+ lines), a `# pyright: ignore[code]` comment on the last line does NOT suppress the error reported on the first line. Pyright attributes the error to the first line of the expression, but the suppress comment is on a later line.

Example of the failing pattern:
```python
if not _HTTPX_SOCKS_AVAILABLE:
    raise ImportError("...")
assert AsyncProxyTransport is not None
proxyUrl = buildProxyUrl(proxyConfig)
mainTransport = AsyncProxyTransport.from_url(  # pyright: ignore[reportOptionalMemberAccess]
    proxyUrl
)
getUpdatesTransport = AsyncProxyTransport.from_url(proxyUrl)  # pyright: ignore[...]
```
→ pyright still reports `"from_url" is not a known attribute of "None"` on the start of the expression.

**Fix**: Use `assert AsyncProxyTransport is not None` before the guarded calls. This narrows the type from `type[AsyncProxyTransport] | None` to `type[AsyncProxyTransport]` for pyright, eliminating the need for `# pyright: ignore` entirely (assuming code is inside a `_HTTPX_SOCKS_AVAILABLE` guard).

title: ProxyHelper.getGlobalProxyConfig() crashes — passes ProxyConfig to fromDict which expects a dict
content:
`ProxyHelper.getGlobalProxyConfig()` in lib/proxy/__init__.py has a runtime bug:

```python
def getGlobalProxyConfig(self) -> ProxyConfig:
    if self.globalProxyConfig is None:
        raise TypeError("need to call setGlobalProxyConfig() first")
    return ProxyConfig.fromDict(self.globalProxyConfig)  # BUG
```

`self.globalProxyConfig` is already a `ProxyConfig` instance (set by `setGlobalProxyConfig()` which does `self.globalProxyConfig = ProxyConfig.fromDict(proxyConfig)`). But `ProxyConfig.fromDict()` expects a `ProxyConfigDict` (a TypedDict) and calls `data.get("enabled")` on it. Since `ProxyConfig` uses `__slots__` and has no `.get()` method, this raises `AttributeError`.

Fix: `return self.globalProxyConfig` directly (no re-wrapping via `fromDict`). The guard clause correctly raises `TypeError` before the buggy line when called before `setGlobalProxyConfig`.

The test suite works around this by patching `getGlobalProxyConfig` on the singleton instance.

title: ProxyConfig.fromDict does NOT default missing "type" to HTTP — defaulting is in getCombined
content:
In the class-based proxy API (`lib/proxy/__init__.py`), `ProxyConfig.fromDict()` does NOT default a missing "type" key to HTTP like the old `resolveProxyConfig()` did. Instead:

- `fromDict(data, useProxy=True)` passes `data.get("type")` straight through — returns `None` if "type" is absent
- `fromDict(data, useProxy=False)` returns `ProxyType.NONE`
- The default-to-HTTP behavior now lives in `getCombined()`: `proxyType=self.type or globalProxyConfig.type or ProxyType.NONE`

This means a `ProxyConfig` with `type=None` is a valid intermediate state. Tests that expect `proxyType == ProxyType.HTTP` directly from `fromDict` are wrong — they must go through `getCombined()` or have a global config set up with HTTP type.

title: ProxyConfig.getProxyURL(maskPassword=True) uses "REDACTED" not "***"
content: In the class-based proxy API (`lib/proxy/__init__.py`), `ProxyConfig.getProxyURL(maskPassword=True)` replaces the password with the literal string `"REDACTED"` in the URL. This differs from the old `maskedProxyUrl()` which used `"***"`.

title: ProxyHelper.setGlobalProxyConfig requires "enabled": True for proxy to activate
content:
`ProxyHelper.setGlobalProxyConfig(proxyConfig)` passes its dict to `ProxyConfig.fromDict()`. Inside `fromDict`, when `useProxy` is not explicitly passed (it defaults to `None`), it falls back to `data.get("enabled") is True`. 

Without `"enabled": True` in the dict:
- `data.get("enabled")` returns `None` (key missing)
- `None is True` evaluates to `False`
- `useProxy` becomes `False`
- `proxyType` is set to `ProxyType.NONE`

So calling `setGlobalProxyConfig({"type": "http", "address": "http://p:80"})` results in a NONE-type global config. The dict must explicitly include `"enabled": True` to get a working proxy config.

title: ProxyConfig constructor raises ValueError for non-NONE type with empty address
content: `ProxyConfig.__init__` validates that when `proxyType` is not `ProxyType.NONE` and not `None`, the `address` must be truthy (non-empty). Otherwise it raises `ValueError("Address is required for non-NONE proxy type")`. This guard makes the empty-address error path in `_buildProxyUrl` (where `urlparse("").hostname` is `None`) unreachable through normal API usage — you literally cannot construct a `ProxyConfig` with HTTP type and empty address. When `proxyType` is `None` (from `fromDict` with no "type" key) or `ProxyType.NONE`, the address is allowed to be empty (wiped to `""` for NONE).

title: ProxyHelper singleton refactoring broke all downstream consumer tests
content:
The refactoring of `lib/proxy` from module-level functions (`resolveProxyConfig`, `buildProxyUrl`, `buildHttpxClientKwargs`) to class-based API (`ProxyConfig`, `ProxyHelper` singleton) broke tests in all downstream consumers:

- `lib/geocode_maps/`
- `lib/openweathermap/`
- `lib/yandex_search/`
- `lib/divination/`
- `lib/ai/` providers

All fail with `TypeError: need to call setGlobalProxyConfig() first`. The old stateless API didn't require any setup; the new `ProxyHelper` singleton requires `ProxyHelper.getInstance().setGlobalProxyConfig(...)` before any API call that resolves proxy config. Every test in consumer libraries needs either:
1. A fixture that sets up ProxyHelper with a NONE-type global config (to disable proxy), or
2. Mocking the proxy resolution.

This should be fixed with a conftest fixture or a global autouse fixture in `tests/conftest.py`.

title: replaceAll can corrupt dict keys when matching string patterns in TypedDict
content: When using `replaceAll` to fix pyright TypedDict type errors by replacing string literal values (e.g. `"type": "http"` → `"type": ProxyType.HTTP`), the replacement pattern can also match dict keys if they happen to have the same string literal structure (e.g. `ProxyType.HTTP: ProxyType.HTTP` instead of `"type": ProxyType.HTTP`). Always verify the replacement context, especially in TypedDict constructors where keys and values may have overlapping patterns.

title: Determining direct vs transitive dependencies: grep for imports
content:
When adding a dependency to the project, determine whether it's a direct or transitive dependency before deciding which file to modify:

1. **`requirements.direct.txt`** — only genuine direct dependencies (packages that are imported anywhere in the codebase via `import X` or `from X import Y`).
2. **`requirements.txt`** (frozen/locked) — contains both direct and transitive dependencies, all with exact pinned versions.

**Procedure to determine if a package is a direct dependency:**
- Run `rg --no-heading "from PACKAGE_NAME\b" --include "*.py"` or `rg --no-heading "import PACKAGE_NAME\b"` across the source tree (excluding `requirements*.txt` and `venv/`).
- If any file directly imports from the package, it's a direct dependency and belongs in `requirements.direct.txt`.
- If no direct imports are found, the package is a transitive dependency (pulled in by another direct dependency) and should only appear in the frozen `requirements.txt`.

**Examples from this codebase:**
- `httpx-socks` — direct dependency, imported via `from httpx_socks import AsyncProxyTransport` in `lib/proxy/__init__.py` → belongs in `requirements.direct.txt`.
- `httpx-sse` — transitive dependency, no direct imports anywhere in the codebase; it's pulled in by `openai` for SSE streaming → stays only in `requirements.txt`.

This prevents adding transitive dependencies to `requirements.direct.txt` and keeps it as an accurate manifest of the project's actual code-level dependencies.

title: Documentation principle: AGENTS.md as error-margin guide, not general reference
content:
AGENTS.md should capture only what an agent would likely get wrong without help — project-specific gotchas, non-obvious conventions, and error-prone patterns. It should NOT contain:

- Generic software engineering advice (DRY, type safety in tests, import placement)
- Rules already stated in other project docs (AGENTS.md hard rules are the canonical source; do not duplicate them in tasks.md)
- Feature-specific implementation details (belong in the subsystem's own docs/code, not in a project-wide gotchas table)

Confirmed during the 2026-05-30 cleanup: removed 4 subsections from tasks.md (§4.2 Import Placement, §4.3 Code Duplication, §4.4 Type Safety in Tests, §4.6 String Enum Conventions) because they were either generic advice or duplicated from AGENTS.md hard rules. Also removed 7 feature-specific gotchas rows (divination and sandbox internals) from the §3 gotchas table.

title: Teamlead memory extraction pattern — extract task-specific sections into memories/*.md
content:
Established documentation architecture pattern for `docs/llm/teamlead-memory.md`:

When `teamlead-memory.md` accumulates task-specific sections (proxy refactoring, test reorganization, etc.), extract them into separate files under `docs/llm/memories/*.md` and keep `teamlead-memory.md` focused on cross-cutting repo-wide rules, workflow lessons, and user preferences.

**Extraction principles codified:**
1. **Cross-cutting repo-wide rules** stay in `teamlead-memory.md` (e.g., user preferences, general gotchas, workflow lessons).
2. **Subsystem-scoped discoveries** go to `docs/llm/memories/*.md` (e.g., proxy-specific conventions, test reorganization details).
3. **Items already codified in AGENTS.md** as hard rules should be removed from `teamlead-memory.md` entirely — no duplication of authoritative rule sources.
4. **"Continued" sections** (e.g., "Repo Facts And Gotchas (continued)") should be merged into the main section, not left as separate headings.
5. **Update `docs/llm/memories/index.md`** with entries for each new memory file, including trigger descriptions for when to read them.
6. **Update the "Task-Specific Memory Files" section** in `teamlead-memory.md` to reference all memory files.

**Session's concrete application:** Extracted `proxy.md` (lines 69-151 of old teamlead-memory) and `test-reorganization.md` (lines 153-166) into `docs/llm/memories/`. Removed rules already in AGENTS.md (StrEnum, no imports in functions, requirements.txt frozen, no PEP 695 type aliases, class-based design). Merged "continued" sections into main sections. teamlead-memory.md went from 166 lines to 64 lines.

title: Avoid line-number and section-number references in docs
content:
File path + line number references (e.g., `main.py:54`) in documentation are fragile because line numbers shift as the codebase evolves. The reference `main.py:54` in AGENTS.md pointed to the bot mode wiring, but it was already stale — the wiring had moved to ~line 78. Use bare file paths or stable function/class anchors instead of line numbers.

Similarly, cross-references by section number (e.g., `see §4.5`) break when sections are renumbered during edits. Prefer section titles or stable anchor IDs.

Fixed during 2026-05-30 cleanup: changed `main.py:54` to bare `main.py` in AGENTS.md, and updated a stale `§4.5` reference to `§4.2` in tasks.md after subsections were removed.

title: Fold tiny single-purpose sections into lists
content: Small, single-purpose sections that only point to another document or serve as a brief transition do not earn their own heading. Fold them into the nearest list or paragraph instead. During the 2026-05-30 cleanup, the standalone "Documentation Maintenance" section in AGENTS.md (2 lines, just pointing to `docs/documentation-review-process.md`) was folded into the "Existing instruction sources" bullet list.

title: ProxyConfig two-level kill-switch: enabled field semantics in getCombined()
content:
ProxyConfig now has an `enabled` field (bool, defaults to False) that implements a two-level kill-switch in `getCombined()`:

1. **Master kill-switch** (global `proxy.enabled = false`): If `globalProxyConfig.enabled` is False, `getCombined()` returns `ProxyConfig(proxyType=ProxyType.NONE)` — NO service uses proxy regardless of per-service flags.
2. **Service kill-switch** (per-service `enabled = false` in proxy subsection): If `self.enabled` is False, `getCombined()` returns a copy of the global config (`globalProxyConfig.copy()`).
3. **Both enabled**: Standard merge behavior — service-level fields override global, `None` fields inherit from global.

Key detail: `fromDict()` uses `data.get("enabled") is True` (strict identity check, not truthiness) so `enabled: False` in TOML is properly recognized as disabled, while a missing `enabled` key returns None → `fromDict` defaults to `False`.

This was added via commit `db60e13` after a code review flagged that the original kill-switch was bypassable by per-service proxy overrides. The global `[proxy].enabled` config key (in `configs/00-defaults/proxy.toml`) is the authoritative kill-switch.

title: fromServiceDict renamed to fromServiceConfig across proxy code and docs
content:
The `ProxyConfig` classmethod `fromServiceDict()` was renamed to `fromServiceConfig()` to better reflect that it takes a service-level config dict (which may contain `use-proxy` and `proxy` sub-keys). All references across the codebase and documentation have been updated:

- `lib/proxy/__init__.py` — method definition and usage
- `tests/lib/test_proxy.py` — test class renamed from `TestProxyConfigFromServiceDict` to `TestProxyConfigFromServiceConfig`
- `docs/llm/configuration.md`, `docs/llm/memories/proxy.md`, `docs/llm/libraries.md`, `docs/llm/services.md` — all occurrences replaced

Zero `fromServiceDict` references remain in any file.

title: ProxyConfig empty-string vs None semantics: empty-string overrides global
content:
In `ProxyConfig.getCombined()`, the fallback logic for `user`/`password` uses `is not None` (not truthiness):

- `None` → inherit from global config
- empty string `""` → explicitly override global with empty (intentionally set to no auth)

This differs from `address` which uses `or` semantics (`self.address or globalProxyConfig.address`), meaning empty-string address falls through to global.

This is by design: setting `user = ""` or `password = ""` in TOML explicitly clears the global proxy credentials for that service. Documented in `ProxyConfig.__init__` docstring and `ProxyConfigDict` TypedDict docstring.

title: Test monkeypatch removal: resetProxyHelper no longer patches getGlobalProxyConfig
content:
The `resetProxyHelper` fixture in `tests/lib/test_proxy.py` previously monkeypatched `ProxyHelper.getGlobalProxyConfig()` with `_patchedGetGlobal` to work around a bug where the real method called `ProxyConfig.fromDict(self.globalProxyConfig)` on an already-instantiated ProxyConfig. The bug was fixed (now `getGlobalProxyConfig()` simply returns `self.globalProxyConfig` directly), so the monkeypatch was removed.

The fixture now just resets the singleton (`ProxyHelper._instance = None`) and sets a default global config with `{"enabled": True}`. Individual tests override via `setGlobalProxyConfig()`. The false bug comment ("the current implementation has a bug...") was also removed.

title: Sqlink proxy config must live under database.providers.<name>.parameters
content:
The SQLink database provider (`internal/database/providers/sqlink.py`) reads proxy settings from its `**kwargs`, which are populated by the `getSqlProvider()` function unpacking `config["parameters"]`. Therefore, for sqlink proxy to work, the `use-proxy` flag and `[proxy]` sub-table must live INSIDE `[database.providers.<name>.parameters]`, not at the provider level.

Correct TOML structure:
```toml
[database.providers.archive]
provider = "sqlink"

[database.providers.archive.parameters]
url = "https://..."
user = "bot"
password = "${DB_PASSWORD}"
database = "archive_db"
use-proxy = true

[database.providers.archive.parameters.proxy]
type = "http"
address = "${DB_PROXY_ADDRESS}"
```

This is documented in `docs/llm/configuration.md` under the `[database]` section.

title: Teamlead reviews can delegate code-review subtasks — learns from residual issues found in Gate 2
content:
A pattern used in this session: the teamlead agent dispatched primary fixes as parallel subtasks (docstrings update, test fix, docs fix), then ran a Gate 2 whole-work code review. The review caught issues the first pass missed: stale `fromServiceDict` references across 5 doc files, wrong ProxyHelper docstrings, inaccurate ProxyConfigDict descriptions, comment typos, and test class name. These were then dispatched as a second round of parallel fixes.

Key lesson: parallel subtask dispatch + whole-work review catches cross-file consistency issues that single-task agents miss. The `code-reviewer` agent with `git diff HEAD...HEAD~1` (or against the base branch) should be invoked after all subtasks complete to catch drift between doc files and code.

title: Service proxy requires `enabled: true` in proxy section to override global in getCombined()
content:
When using `fromServiceConfig()` with `use-proxy: True`, the service proxy section must include `"enabled": True` for the service to override the global proxy config in `getCombined()`.

Why: `fromServiceConfig()` passes `useProxy=True` to `fromDict(proxyDict, useProxy=True)`. Inside `fromDict`, `enabled = data.get("enabled") is True`. Since typical proxy sections (e.g., `{"address": "http://s:80"}`) don't include an `enabled` key, `data.get("enabled")` returns `None`, `None is True` is `False`, so the resulting `ProxyConfig` has `enabled=False`.

With `enabled=False`, `getCombined()` treats the service config as "disabled" and returns a copy of the global config — service-level fields (address, type, credentials) do NOT override global. This is the service-level kill-switch behavior.

Example that fails:
```python
ProxyConfig.fromServiceConfig({"use-proxy": True, "proxy": {"address": "http://s:80"}})
# → ProxyConfig(enabled=False, address="http://s:80")
# getCombined() returns globalProxyConfig.copy(), not merged with service address
```

Fix: add `"enabled": True` to the proxy section:
```python
ProxyConfig.fromServiceConfig({"use-proxy": True, "proxy": {"address": "http://s:80", "enabled": True}})
# → ProxyConfig(enabled=True, address="http://s:80")
# getCombined() merges: service address overrides global
```

This was discovered when three `tests/lib/test_proxy.py` tests (`test_serviceProxy_usesServiceAddress`, `test_serviceProxy_overridesGlobalType`, `test_serviceProxy_overridesCredentials`) failed after the production code was updated with the two-level kill-switch.

title: ProxyConfig.toKwargs() creates a new AsyncProxyTransport per call for SOCKS5 — no caching
_buildProxyUrl doesn't validate proxy address scheme — unrecognized schemes pass silently
content:
`ProxyConfig._buildProxyUrl()` in `lib/proxy/__init__.py` validates that the parsed address has a hostname (raises `ValueError` if missing), but does NOT validate the URL scheme. If a user configures `address = "https://proxy:8080"` or `address = "socks4://proxy:1080"` (typo: `socks4` instead of `socks5`), the address is accepted without warning and the wrong scheme is used in the built proxy URL.

The existing guard `if not parsed.hostname: raise ValueError(...)` handles fully unparseable addresses like `proxy:8080` (without a scheme prefix), since `urlparse` treats `proxy` as the scheme and `hostname` becomes `None`. But it does NOT catch:
- Recognized-but-unsupported schemes like `https://` (user likely meant `http://`)
- Typos like `socks4://` (user likely meant `socks5://`)
- Future proxy protocol additions where a typo could silently fall through

To add validation, the canonical approach would be to check the parsed scheme in `_buildProxyUrl` or `ProxyConfig.__init__` against a whitelist of supported schemes (`http`, `socks5`).

title: sqlink.py uses ProxyConfig.fromDict() directly instead of fromServiceDict() — style inconsistency
content:
The `SQLinkProvider` in `internal/database/providers/sqlink.py` uses `ProxyConfig.fromDict()` directly to construct its proxy configuration:

```python
self._proxy = ProxyConfig.fromDict(
    proxy if proxy is not None else {},
    useProxy=kwargs.get("use-proxy") is True,
)
```

This differs from every other proxy-using service in the codebase, which use `ProxyConfig.fromServiceDict(config)` — a factory method that reads both `use-proxy` and `proxy` keys from the same config dict.

The reason for the difference is structural: sqlink's proxy config keys (`proxy`, `use-proxy`) come from separate constructor parameters rather than a single config dict. The `proxy` parameter is passed as a `ProxyConfigDict` (the `[database.providers.default.proxy]` sub-section), and `use-proxy` is extracted from `kwargs` (which is the full provider config dict).

Functionally equivalent, but stylistically inconsistent. If refactoring to match the rest of the codebase, the sqlink constructor could accept the full provider config dict and call `ProxyConfig.fromServiceDict(config)` like every other service.

title: ProxyConfig.__repr__/__str__ mask passwords as '***' (not 'REDACTED')
content:
`ProxyConfig.__repr__` and `__str__` (which delegates to `__repr__`) mask non-empty passwords as `'***'` in the output. Empty or `None` passwords render verbatim (via `!r`). This is distinct from `getProxyURL(maskPassword=True)` which uses the literal string `"REDACTED"`.

Implementation in `lib/proxy/__init__.py`:
```python
f"password={'***' if self.password else self.password!r}, enabled={self.enabled!r})"
```

The `!r` applies to the entire ternary expression — non-empty → `'***'`, `None` → `None`, `""` → `""`.

title: ProxyConfig.__repr__ masks password in log output
content:
`ProxyConfig.__repr__` in `lib/proxy/__init__.py` now masks the password field: non-empty passwords render as `'***'`, while falsy values (None, empty string) render normally via `repr`. Applied by changing line 209 from `f"password={self.password!r}"` to `f"password={'***' if self.password else self.password!r}"`.

This prevents credential leakage in logs, exception tracebacks, and test failure output. `__str__` delegates to `__repr__` and inherits the masking automatically.

Note: This is a separate concern from `ProxyConfig.getProxyURL(maskPassword=True)` which uses the literal string `"REDACTED"` for URL-based masking — there are intentionally two different mask values (`"***"` vs `"REDACTED"`) depending on the output context (repr vs URL).

title: Address validation removed from ProxyConfig.__init__ — deferred to _buildProxyUrl
content:
The `ValueError` guard that validated `address` in `ProxyConfig.__init__` when `proxyType != NONE` was completely removed. Previously the constructor would reject `ProxyConfig(ProxyType.HTTP, address="")` with `ValueError`. Now empty addresses are accepted at construction time — the error surfaces later only when `getProxyURL()` or `toKwargs()` calls `_buildProxyUrl()`.

This was required because disabled default configs (`enabled=false, type=http, address=""`) would crash at startup if the constructor validated address without checking `enabled`. The fix also added an `enabled` field (bool, default `True`) to `ProxyConfig`.

Key: intermediate states like `ProxyConfig(type=HTTP, address="", enabled=False)` are now valid. Stale docstrings that say "Must be provided" for address are wrong if they reference constructor-side validation.

title: Pre-existing test failure on add-proxy-support branch: test_emptyAddress_causesValueError
content:
On the `add-proxy-support` branch, the test `test_emptyAddress_causesValueError` in `tests/lib/test_proxy.py` is a pre-existing failure. The test expects `ProxyConfig(ProxyType.HTTP, "")` to raise `ValueError`, but the constructor validation (`if proxyType != ProxyType.NONE and proxyType is not None: if not address: raise ValueError(...)`) has already been removed from the working tree on this branch. This failure is unrelated to any new changes and was present before modifications to `__repr__` password masking.

40 of 41 proxy tests pass; this single failure is pre-existing and should be fixed separately if the constructor validation removal was intentional.

title: Telegram bot: explicit getCombined() call before type match is intentional for type dispatch
content:
In `internal/bot/telegram/application.py`, `ProxyConfig.fromServiceConfig(botConfig).getCombined()` is called explicitly before the `match proxyConfig.type:` block rather than deferring to `getProxyURL()` or `toKwargs()` (which also call `getCombined()` internally). This is intentional because the Telegram bot needs the resolved `proxyConfig.type` to determine which code path to take:

- **HTTP**: uses `appBuilder.proxy(proxyUrl).get_updates_proxy(proxyUrl)` — requires a raw URL string
- **SOCKS5**: uses `appBuilder.request(request).get_updates_request(request)` — requires `HTTPXRequest` with transport kwargs

The subsequent internal `getCombined()` calls within `getProxyURL()`/`toKwargs()` are harmless no-ops since the config is already combined.

Also: two separate `AsyncProxyTransport` instances are created for SOCKS5 (one for main client, one for `get_updates`) intentionally, so the two polling loops don't share connection state.

title: ProxyConfig.__repr__ and __str__ leak password in plain text
content:
The code-reviewer agent identified a security vulnerability in `lib/proxy/__init__.py`: `ProxyConfig.__repr__()` includes `self.password` in plain text. Since `__str__()` delegates to `__repr__()`, both methods expose the proxy password. This means any code that logs a `ProxyConfig` object via `logger.debug(f"config: {config}")`, exception formatting, or `str(config)` will leak credentials to logs, error trackers, and console output.

Only `getProxyURL(maskPassword=True)` currently masks the password (using the literal string `"REDACTED"`). The `__repr__` method should mask the password similarly to avoid accidental credential leakage during debugging or logging.

Location: `lib/proxy/__init__.py`, lines ~209-213 (in `ProxyConfig.__repr__`).

Fix: Replace `self.password!r` with a masked version in both `__repr__` and `__str__`:

title: ProxyConfig __init__ ValueError address guard removed in fix commit 57c651f
content:
The `ValueError` guard in `ProxyConfig.__init__` that prevented construction with `proxyType != NONE` and empty address was **removed** in commit `57c651f` ("Fixed issues, found by reviewer"). The original guard (from commit `314b453`) was:

```python
if proxyType != ProxyType.NONE and proxyType is not None:
    if not address:
        raise ValueError("address should be valid proxy address in case of proxyType != NONE")
```

With the removal, a disabled default config `{enabled: false, type: "http", address: ""}` no longer crashes on startup. The fix also:
- Added the `enabled` field (bool, default `True`) as the 5th slot in `ProxyConfig.__init__`, alongside `proxyType`, `address`, `user`, `password`.
- Added `ProxyConfig.__repr__`, `__str__`, and `copy()` methods.
- In `__init__`, when `proxyType == ProxyType.NONE`, address/user/password are wiped to `""` (lines 130-134 in current code).
- The `getCombined()` method returns `ProxyConfig(proxyType=ProxyType.NONE)` early when the global config is disabled, preventing the empty address from reaching `_buildProxyUrl`.

Note: The existing memory "ProxyConfig constructor raises ValueError for non-NONE type with empty address" (d4d20065) documents the ORIGINAL behavior that has since been removed. This memory supersedes it.

title: Disabled default proxy config (enabled=false, type=http, address="") must not crash at startup
content:
The default proxy config `configs/00-defaults/proxy.toml` sets `enabled=false, type="http", address=""`. The application must be able to start with this config without errors.

The fix: removed the `ValueError` from `ProxyConfig.__init__` (which previously rejected non-NONE types with empty address), and added an `enabled` field. The `getCombined()` method checks global `enabled` first — when disabled, it returns `ProxyConfig(proxyType=ProxyType.NONE)` without ever calling `_buildProxyUrl()` with the empty address.

A regression test `test_disabledWithEmptyAddress_doesNotRaiseValueError` was added to verify `fromDict({"enabled": False, "type": ProxyType.HTTP, "address": ""})` does not raise `ValueError`, returns `type=HTTP, enabled=False, address=""`.

title: Missing regression test: disabled default proxy config fromDict should not crash
content:
There is NO test that explicitly verifies the specific startup crash scenario: calling `ProxyConfig.fromDict({"enabled": False, "type": "http", "address": ""})` or `ProxyConfig(proxyType=ProxyType.HTTP, address="", enabled=False)`. 

The existing test `test_useProxyDefault_enabledFalse_usesNONE` tests `enabled=False` with `ProxyType.HTTP` but with a **non-empty** address, so it doesn't exercise the exact empty-address code path that caused the original crash.

Recommended regression test additions:
1. `fromDict` with `enabled=False, type="http", address=""` — should create ProxyConfig successfully without ValueError
2. `fromDict` with `useProxy=None` (global config path), `enabled=False, type="http", address=""` — the exact startup path
3. `setGlobalProxyConfig({"enabled": False, "type": "http", "address": "", "user": "", "password": ""})` — should not crash
4. `getCombined()` when global config has `enabled=False, type="http", address=""` — should return `ProxyType.NONE`

title: EditAccessManager: all slash-commands bypass chatId sign-based type inference
content: `EditAccessManager` in `lib/bot_acl/edit_access_manager.py` determines whether the user is in a group chat or private chat using the `chatId` sign heuristic (`chatId < 0` = group). However, all slash-commands (handled by `/command` handlers registered in `HandlersManager`) completely bypass this module — they go through `CommandHandler` which does its own permission checks before reaching the handler that might use `EditAccessManager`. This means `EditAccessManager`'s `getChatType(chatId)` and sign-based inference only applies to **inline buttons** and **non-command messages** that go through the catch-all handler chain.

title: Test gap: getCombined() kill-switch paths have zero dedicated tests
content:
The `getCombined()` method in `ProxyConfig` (`lib/proxy/__init__.py`) has two code paths with zero dedicated test coverage:

1. **Master kill-switch** (line 301-302): When `globalProxyConfig.enabled` is False, `getCombined()` returns `ProxyConfig(proxyType=ProxyType.NONE)` — no service uses the proxy regardless of per-service config.
2. **Service kill-switch** (line 304-305): When `self.enabled` is False (service-level), `getCombined()` returns `globalProxyConfig.copy()` — the service inherits the global config.

All existing tests in `TestProxyConfigGetCombined` use a global config with `enabled: True` and never test a service config with `enabled: False`. These code paths are exercised only indirectly through other tests that create configs without `enabled: True`, causing full global inheritance.

Additionally, several tests in `TestProxyConfigGetCombined` (e.g., `test_serviceProxy_inheritsGlobalType`, `test_serviceProxy_inheritsCredentials`, `test_serviceProxy_emptyAddress_fallsToGlobal`) create service configs via `fromServiceConfig` without `enabled: True` in the proxy section. This causes `enabled=False`, which makes `getCombined()` return the global config entirely (service kill-switch). The tests pass, but they verify "inherit everything from global" rather than "inherit specific fields from global." The service's own proxy settings (e.g., `address: "http://s:80"`) are silently discarded.

title: SQLinkProvider resolves proxy URL lazily in connect() not __init__()
content:
The `SQLinkProvider` in `internal/database/providers/sqlink.py` stores a `ProxyConfig` object (not a resolved URL string) in `self._proxy`. The proxy URL is resolved lazily by calling `self._proxy.getProxyURL()` inside `connect()`, just before passing it to `sqlink.asyncConnect(proxy=proxyUrl)`.

Rationale: This allows the global proxy configuration (set via `ProxyHelper.setGlobalProxyConfig()`) to be changed after the `SQLinkProvider` is constructed but before the first connection attempt. If `getProxyURL()` were called eagerly in `__init__()`, the global config would be captured at construction time and late-set changes would be ignored.

Key implementation details:
- `__init__` does `self._proxy = ProxyConfig.fromDict(...)` — no `.getProxyURL()` call
- `connect()` does `proxyUrl = self._proxy.getProxyURL()` then passes `proxy=proxyUrl`
- The `_proxy` slot stores a `ProxyConfig` object, not a string

title: ProxyConfig.fromDict docstring still says "type NONE if no address" but that's stale
content:
The `ProxyConfig.fromDict()` docstring in `lib/proxy/__init__.py:163-164` still says:

```
Returns:
    A ProxyConfig with the specified settings, or type NONE if
    ``useProxy`` is False or no address is provided.
```

The "or no address is provided" clause describes the OLD behavior where `fromDict` would force type to NONE when address was empty. Since the `ValueError` guard in `__init__` was removed (to allow intermediate states like `type=HTTP, address="", enabled=False`), `fromDict({"type": ProxyType.HTTP, "address": ""})` now returns `ProxyConfig(type=HTTP, address="")`, NOT `ProxyConfig(type=NONE)`. Only the `useProxy=False` path forces NONE.

This docstring was accurate before the validation-removal fix but was not updated alongside the code change. Any agent or developer reading it will get the wrong impression of `fromDict` behavior.

title: `toKwargs()` creates new AsyncProxyTransport on every call — SOCKS5 resource leak for per-request services
content:
`ProxyConfig.toKwargs()` in `lib/proxy/__init__.py` creates a new `AsyncProxyTransport` from URL on every invocation when proxy type is SOCKS5. For per-request services (OpenWeatherMap, GeocodeMaps, Yandex Search) that create a new `httpx.AsyncClient` per HTTP request, this means a new transport per request — leaking connection pools and potentially exhausting file descriptors.

Unlike the PTB telegram bot (which creates 2 `HTTPXRequest` instances that share the same transport from a single `toKwargs()` call — already captured in memory e3559ba7), the per-request services call `toKwargs()` fresh each time, creating a new transport every time. For HTTP proxy type, `toKwargs()` returns a simple `proxy` string — no leak there. Only SOCKS5 is affected.

**Suggested fix from code review:** Either (a) cache the transport in `ProxyConfig`/`ProxyHelper` so it's created once and reused, or (b) document that SOCKS5 callers must manage transport lifecycle themselves.

title: SQLinkProvider class docstring says ``proxy`` attribute is URL string, but stored as ProxyConfig
content:
In `internal/database/providers/sqlink.py`, the `SQLinkProvider` class docstring `Attributes` section (~line 35-38) says:

```
proxy: Optional proxy URL string for HTTP/HTTPS routing through a proxy.
    Stored as a :class:`~lib.proxy.ProxyConfig` and resolved lazily via
    :meth:`~lib.proxy.ProxyConfig.getProxyURL` at connection time.
```

Two issues:
1. The attribute name in `__slots__` is `_proxy` (private), not `proxy`. The class-level `Attributes` section should document `_proxy`.
2. The first sentence describes it as a "proxy URL string" but the stored value is a `ProxyConfig` instance (not a string). The lazy resolution change (session 2026-05-30) converted it from an eagerly-resolved URL string to a stored `ProxyConfig` object, but the first line of the docstring wasn't corrected.

This is a documentation inconsistency that would mislead any developer reading the class docstring.

title: Missing test: ProxyConfig.__repr__ password masking has no automated test
content:
The `ProxyConfig.__repr__` method in `lib/proxy/__init__.py` masks non-empty passwords as `'***'`, while `None` and empty strings render verbatim. This is a security-relevant behavior, but there are zero automated tests in `tests/lib/test_proxy.py` verifying it.

Specific cases that should be tested:
- Non-empty password (e.g. `"secret"`) → output contains `password='***'`
- `None` password → output contains `password=None`
- Empty string password → output contains `password=''`
- `__str__` delegates to `__repr__` (already true via default `__str__`, but worth verifying explicitly)

Without a regression test, a future change that "simplifies" the ternary expression (e.g. reverting to `f"password={self.password!r}"`) would silently leak passwords in log output. See project AGENTS.md rule: "Regression tests on every bug fix" — while repr masking started as a feature change, its security sensitivity qualifies it for the same treatment.

title: ProxyConfig.__repr__ docstring doesn't mention password masking
content:
The `ProxyConfig.__repr__()` docstring in `lib/proxy/__init__.py` (~line 199) says:

```
Returns:
    A string like ``ProxyConfig(proxyType=..., address=..., ..., enabled=...)``
    suitable for debugging.
```

It does not document that non-empty passwords are masked as `'***'` in the output. This is a security-relevant behavior that developers need to be aware of — especially since `__str__` delegates to `__repr__`, meaning `str(proxyConfig)` and f-strings like `f"{proxyConfig}"` are also affected.

The proxy task memory (`docs/llm/memories/proxy.md`) documents this detail, but the inline docstring is the first place a developer will look. The docstring should include a note like: "Non-empty passwords are masked as `'***'`; None and empty-string passwords render verbatim."

title: `getCombined()` tightly coupled to ProxyHelper singleton — makes ProxyConfig hard to test/compose in isolation
content:
`ProxyConfig.getCombined()` in `lib/proxy/__init__.py:298` directly calls `ProxyHelper.getInstance().getGlobalProxyConfig()` to fetch the global proxy configuration for merge logic. This makes every `ProxyConfig` instance implicitly coupled to a global singleton. Any code path that calls `getCombined()`, `getProxyURL()`, or `toKwargs()` on a `ProxyConfig` must have `ProxyHelper` singleton initialized first, or it raises `TypeError`. 

The `fromServiceConfig()` factory (which calls `fromDict()` with defaults) does NOT have this coupling — it only manifests in `getCombined()`. This makes `ProxyConfig` harder to test in isolation and harder to reuse for purposes unrelated to the global proxy.

The existing `test_proxy.py` test fixture must explicitly initialize `ProxyHelper.getInstance()` before every test method, and the autouse `resetProxyHelperSingleton` fixture in `conftest.py` resets it between every test.

**Suggested fix (from code review):** Make `getCombined()` accept an optional `globalConfig` parameter so callers can explicitly inject a global config. Fall back to `ProxyHelper.getInstance()` only when not provided.

title: ProxyKwargs.transport annotation causes runtime ImportError when httpx_socks not installed
content:
`ProxyKwargs` TypedDict in `lib/proxy/__init__.py` declares `transport: AsyncProxyTransport` as a field annotation. Unlike regular class annotations (which PEP 563 / `from __future__ import annotations` converts to strings), TypedDict field annotations ARE evaluated at class creation time because `TypedDict.__init_subclass__` reads `__annotations__` to define the struct fields. This means:

- Even though pyright resolves `AsyncProxyTransport` from the `try` branch of the conditional import (as documented in existing memory about the conditional import pattern), **Python WILL raise `NameError` at module import time** if `httpx_socks` is not installed.
- The `from __future__ import annotations` directive does NOT help here — TypedDict's metaclass evaluates annotations eagerly regardless.
- The fix is to either: (a) use a quoted forward-reference `"AsyncProxyTransport"` in the annotation, (b) type the field as `Any` with a comment, or (c) ensure `httpx_socks` is always installed when proxy support is enabled (it's now a direct dependency in `requirements.direct.txt`).

This is a distinct issue from pyright type resolution: pyright can handle it fine, but the Python runtime cannot.

title: requirements.txt stale after adding httpx-socks to requirements.direct.txt — lockfile regeneration needed
content:
After adding `httpx-socks[asyncio]==0.11.0` to `requirements.direct.txt`, the `requirements.txt` lockfile was NOT regenerated via `freeze-requirements`. This means `make install` (which installs from `requirements.txt`) on a clean checkout will NOT include `httpx-socks` or its transitive dependencies (`python-socks`, `async-socks`). The project convention (documented in AGENTS.md) is that `requirements.txt` is a frozen/locked file regenerated from `requirements.direct.txt` — NEVER edited by hand.

When adding or changing dependencies in `requirements.direct.txt`, the lockfile must be regenerated so fresh installs work. At time of this finding, the branch's `requirements.txt` contains `httpx==0.28.1` but no `httpx-socks` or `python-socks` lines.

title: Per-service proxy override examples in config docs and developer guide missing required `enabled = true`
content:
The `getCombined()` method in `ProxyConfig` requires `enabled = true` inside a service's `[service.proxy]` sub-section for the service-level proxy to override the global proxy config. Without it, the service proxy section is silently ignored and the global proxy is used instead.

However, the per-service override examples in:
1. `configs/00-defaults/00-config.toml` (comments showing `[openweathermap.proxy]` and `[yandex-search.proxy]`)
2. `docs/developer-guide.md` (the proxy configuration section showing TOML examples)
3. `docs/llm/configuration.md` (proxy configuration table and examples)

...all omit `enabled = true` in their per-service override examples. Users following these examples will think they configured a service-specific proxy override, but it will be silently ignored.

For example, the correct override TOML structure is:
```toml
[openweathermap]
use-proxy = true

[openweathermap.proxy]
enabled = true       # REQUIRED for override to take effect
type = "socks5"
address = "${OWM_SOCKS5}"
```

Without `enabled = true`, the `[openweathermap.proxy]` section does nothing.

title: ProxyConfig.__init__ silently coerces None address to "" — validation deferred to _buildProxyUrl() call time
content:
In `ProxyConfig.__init__()` (`lib/proxy/__init__.py:138`), the `address` field defaults to `None` but is silently coerced to `""` via `address or ""`. This means when `proxyType` is `HTTP` or `SOCKS5` and `address` is missing/None, the object is constructed without error. The `ValueError` is only raised later when `_buildProxyUrl()` is called (at proxy usage time, not construction time).

This defers error detection: a misconfigured proxy (missing address with non-NONE type) will not fail at config parsing/startup, but potentially minutes later when the first outbound HTTP request triggers `_buildProxyUrl()`. The test `test_emptyAddress_acceptedInConstructor` explicitly validates this deferred-validation behavior.

**Impact:** Configuration typos in TOML (e.g., missing `address` key) won't surface until the service actually tries to use the proxy. The error will manifest as a `ValueError` at first request time rather than at startup.

title: Scripts reproduce_layout_extraction.py and reproduce_llm_dialog.py missing llmManager.aclose() on shutdown
content:
Two utility/debug scripts initialize `LLMManager` with proxy support but do not call `await llmManager.aclose()` during cleanup:

1. `scripts/reproduce_layout_extraction.py:314` — creates `LLMManager(configManager.getModelsConfig())` but only closes the database at line 437
2. `scripts/reproduce_llm_dialog.py:292` — same pattern, only closes database at line 471

When proxy is enabled for LLM providers, the `LLMManager` may hold proxied `httpx.AsyncClient` instances inside OpenAI-compatible providers. Without calling `aclose()`, these clients and their underlying transport objects (including SOCKS5 `AsyncProxyTransport`) are never explicitly closed, potentially leaking file descriptors and connections.

A prior memory (`d0675835`) documents that `run_llm_debug_query.py` and `check_image_parsing.py` also bypass proxy. These two scripts (`reproduce_layout_extraction.py`, `reproduce_llm_dialog.py`) are a separate issue — they DO configure proxy via `ProxyHelper.getInstance().setGlobalProxyConfig()` before creating `LLMManager`, but fail to clean up. The fix is to add `await llmManager.aclose()` before `await db.manager.closeAll()` in both scripts.

title: `fromServiceConfig` uses `is True` strict check — fragile if called programmatically with non-bool truthy values
content:
`ProxyConfig.fromServiceConfig()` at `lib/proxy/__init__.py:242` uses strict identity check: `useProxy=data.get("use-proxy", False) is True`. This works correctly with TOML-sourced configs (where `true` becomes Python `True`), but would silently treat truthy non-bool values (like `1` or `"true"`) as `False`.

The project's config is exclusively TOML, so this is not a runtime bug in production. But if `fromServiceConfig` is ever called programmatically from test code or future API consumers with a non-bool truthy value, proxy would be silently disabled.

**Suggestion (from code review):** Use `bool(data.get("use-proxy", False))` for robustness, or document that the `is True` check is intentional for TOML strictness.

title: conftest.py autouse fixture sets proxy enabled=False — tests needing proxy must explicitly override
content:
The `resetProxyHelperSingleton` autouse fixture in `tests/conftest.py` resets `ProxyHelper._instance = None` and sets global proxy config to `{"enabled": False}` between every test. This prevents tests from crashing when code paths call `getGlobalProxyConfig()` unnecessarily. However, this means ANY test that needs proxy enabled must explicitly call `ProxyHelper.getInstance().setGlobalProxyConfig({"enabled": True, "type": "http", "address": "http://proxy:80", "user": "", "password": ""})` as part of its setup.

The `test_proxy.py` file has its own `resetProxyHelper` fixture that correctly sets up proxy config for its isolated tests. Other consumer tests (OpenWeatherMap, GeocodeMaps, Yandex Search, etc.) that need proxy must follow the same pattern. Missing explicit `setGlobalProxyConfig()` will silently result in proxy being disabled regardless of what the test's service config specifies.

title: make check is lint+format only, not tests — CI separate gate
content:
The `make check` target in the Makefile runs `make lint` (flake8 + isort --check + pyright) followed by `black --check --diff .`. It does NOT run tests.

CI pipeline (`.sourcecraft/ci.yaml`) runs `make check && make test` as two separate gates. Running `make check` alone does not verify tests pass; running `make test` alone does not verify formatting/lint.

Currently undocumented in AGENTS.md (which documents `make format lint` and `make test` separately but does not mention the `make check` shorthand or the CI two-gate pattern). An agent told to "check code quality" or "verify everything is clean" should run both `make check` and `make test` (or equivalently `make format lint` then `make test`) to match the CI pipeline.

title: lib/divination design: BaseDivinationSystem + subclass pattern for extensibility
content:
The divination feature uses a class hierarchy in `lib/divination/` with `BaseDivinationSystem` as the abstract base class and concrete subclasses like `TarotSystem`, `RunesSystem` for each divination method. This design makes adding future systems (I-Ching, Lenormand, Geomancy) a matter of writing a new subclass + deck data file.

Key design constraints:
- `lib/divination` imports from `lib/ai` only (e.g. `ModelMessage`) — it MUST NOT import from `internal/services/llm`. The handler is the sole caller of `LLMService.generateText`/`generateImage`.
- No `prompts.py` in `lib/divination` — prompts are owned by chat settings + config defaults, not by library code. `lib/divination` only fills template placeholders.
- Single `DivinationHandler` in `internal/bot/common/handlers/divination.py` registers both tarot and runes slash commands (aliases: `/taro`, `/tarot`, `/таро`, `/runes`, `/rune`, `/руны`).

title: Deck/symbol naming: English source-of-truth + localization layer
content: Divination card/rune names use English as the canonical source of truth (stored in `Symbol.name`). A separate localization layer in `lib/divination/localization.py` holds `SYMBOL_NAMES`, `POSITION_NAMES`, `LAYOUT_NAMES` dicts keyed by English name → `{lang: localised}`, plus a `tr()` helper that falls back to English. Only Russian (`"ru"`) is populated in v1. This avoids bilingual data in the core Symbol dataclass and makes adding languages purely data-entry work.

title: Divination drawing: use random.sample wrapped in isolated method
content: `drawSymbols()` in `lib/divination/drawing.py` uses `random.sample(deck, n)` for drawing without replacement (all cards unique). This is the simplest correct implementation. The function is a standalone public method so the algorithm can be swapped later without changing callers. Per-draw 50/50 reversed flag applied for tarot (not for runes).

title: Divination prompts live in chat settings + default config, not in Python
content:
All prompt templates for divination are stored as `ChatSettingsKey` entries with defaults shipped in `configs/00-defaults/` under `[bot.defaults]`. Four new keys:
- `TAROT_SYSTEM_PROMPT`
- `RUNES_SYSTEM_PROMPT`
- `DIVINATION_USER_PROMPT_TEMPLATE` (placeholders: `{userName}`, `{question}`, `{layoutName}`, `{positionsBlock}`, `{cardsBlock}`)
- `DIVINATION_IMAGE_PROMPT_TEMPLATE` (placeholders: `{layoutName}`, `{spreadDescription}`, `{styleHint}`)

No `prompts.py` module in `lib/divination`. The library only fills template strings. Image prompt is NOT a config-level string (no `image-style-hint` config key); it follows the same chat-settings pattern.

title: Divination persistence: composite PK (chat_id, message_id), linked via media_attachments
content:
Migration 014 creates a `divinations` table with:
- Composite primary key `(chat_id, message_id)` — no AUTOINCREMENT, matches `chat_messages` pattern.
- `media_id TEXT` linking to `media_attachments.file_unique_id` for the generated image (FK by convention, same pattern as `chat_messages.media_id`).
- No separate `idx_divinations_created` index — only `(chat_id, user_id, created_at)` index.
- Image bytes stored via the existing media pipeline (`StorageService` + `media_attachments`), not in a custom storage path.

title: Divination handler: gated, TOOLS category, dynamic help, unknown layout stubbed
content:
- Handlers use `CommandCategory.TOOLS` (not `AI`), matching `WeatherHandler`/`MediaHandler` pattern.
- Gated by `[divination] enabled = false` in defaults; conditional registration mirrors `WeatherHandler`.
- Help text is dynamically built from `availableLayouts()` — adding a layout auto-extends `/help`. Unknown layout names also list available ones in the error reply.
- Unknown-layout resolution via LLM is **stubbed** (`_llmGetUnknownLayoutShape()` logs and returns None) pending `lib/ai` structured-output support. A TODO references the gap.
- Reversals: enabled by default for tarot, disabled for runes (Elder Futhark tradition).
- `chatLanguage` is NOT used — prompt tells the model to reply in the language of the user's question.

title: Divination LLM tools: text-only by default, image via flag, reuse rate limiter
content: LLM tools (`do_taro_reading`, `do_runes_reading`) are registered in step 2. They default to text-only responses. An `image-generation = true/false` config flag gates image generation for tool invocations. Rate limiting reuses the existing `LLM_RATELIMITER` chat setting (same queue as `/draw`). No separate `/lastreadings` command in v1, though history is persisted.

title: Plans go in docs/plans/ with a full doc + a companion brief
content:
Architecture/design documents for planned features follow a two-doc convention:
1. **Full plan** — `docs/plans/<feature>-plan.md` — detailed design with architecture diagrams, data types, migration schemas, testing strategy, implementation order.
2. **Brief companion** — `docs/plans/<feature>-plan-brief.md` — quick-reference overview (what, where, layouts, config, testing, steps, out-of-scope).

This pattern was established for the divination handler plan.

title: SQL portability: all SQL must be cross-RDBMS portable
content:
The Gromozeka project uses SQLite in production today but enforces that all SQL must remain portable across SQLite, PostgreSQL, and MySQL. The provider abstraction layer (`BaseSQLProvider`) provides dialect-neutral methods that must be used instead of raw SQL:

- Use `provider.execute` / `executeFetchOne` / `executeFetchAll` / `batchExecute` / `upsert` instead of direct `sqlite3` calls.
- Use `provider.upsert(table, values, conflictColumns, updateExpressions=...)` with `ExcludedValue` marker instead of hand-written `ON CONFLICT ... DO UPDATE`.
- Use `provider.applyPagination(query, limit, offset)` instead of appending `LIMIT ... OFFSET ...` manually.
- Use `provider.getTextType(maxLength=...)` for DDL migration text columns.
- Use `provider.getCaseInsensitiveComparison(column, param)` instead of `COLLATE NOCASE` or `LOWER()` directly.

The provider factory in `internal/database/providers/__init__.py` currently only registers `sqlite3` and `sqlink`; `mysql.py` and `postgresql.py` providers exist but are not yet selectable. Even so, all emitted SQL must stay portable.

A deep reference exists at `docs/sql-portability-guide.md`.

title: No AUTOINCREMENT: composite natural keys preferred
content:
The project strictly avoids `AUTOINCREMENT` (SQLite), `AUTO_INCREMENT` (MySQL), and `SERIAL`/`BIGSERIAL` (PostgreSQL) because each RDBMS spells auto-incrementing primary keys differently. Instead, in order of preference:

1. **Composite natural key** from existing columns: `PRIMARY KEY (chat_id, message_id)`, `PRIMARY KEY (namespace, key)`, `PRIMARY KEY (chat_id, user_id)`. This is the dominant pattern in existing migrations (001-013).
2. **Single natural key** with one external ID: `file_unique_id TEXT PRIMARY KEY`, `chat_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY`.
3. **Application-generated UUID / ULID** stored as `TEXT PRIMARY KEY NOT NULL` (see `delayed_tasks.id` in migration_001/013). Generate in Python before insert; never delegate ID generation to the DB.

Confirmed by grep across all 13 migrations — no existing table uses `AUTOINCREMENT` in any form. This rule was codified in AGENTS.md as a hard requirement.

title: DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP banned for cross-DB compatibility
content: Migration 013 explicitly removed `DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP` from every table. The syntax differs across RDBMS and causes portability issues. Application code sets `created_at` / `updated_at` timestamps explicitly instead. This is documented in `docs/llm/database.md` §7 and codified in AGENTS.md as a hard rule.

title: Portable column types and parameter conventions
content:
For cross-RDBMS portability in Gromozeka:
- Stick to portable column types in migrations: `TEXT`, `INTEGER`, `REAL`, `TIMESTAMP`, `BOOLEAN` (stored as int 0/1 via `convertToSQLite` in `internal/database/providers/utils.py`).
- Store JSON as `TEXT`; do not use SQLite's `JSON1` extension functions.
- Booleans cross the wire as 0/1; do not compare to `TRUE`/`FALSE` literals in SQL.
- Use `?` placeholders consistently in queries (not `%s` or `:name`).
- Do not use `COLLATE NOCASE`; use `LOWER(column) = LOWER(?)` for case-insensitive comparisons instead.

title: Teamlead agent: Prime Directive — Delegate Everything, never execute work directly
content:
The teamlead agent (`.opencode/agents/teamlead.md`) was rewritten on 2026-05-31 to enshrine a strict "delegate everything" role. Key design decisions:

- **Prime Directive**: The teamlead NEVER performs substantive work directly — no code edits, no command execution, no codebase browsing, no web fetches. Only exception is maintaining `docs/llm/teamlead-memory.md`.
- **Intentional permission constraints**: `bash: deny`, `webfetch: deny`, `edit: deny` (except teamlead-memory.md), `write: deny` (except teamlead-memory.md) are explicitly referenced in the prompt as intentional reinforcement of the role, not incidental restrictions.
- **Conflict resolution rule**: Conflicts are reconciled by re-delegating with clarifying context, never by patching results personally.
- **"Never substitute yourself" rule**: Even if a task "seems easy," it must be delegated to a specialist. Judging something as "small enough to do myself" is considered almost always wrong.
- **Decision framework addition**: Before responding, question #6: "Did I do any of this work myself that should have been delegated?" If yes, stop and delegate.
- **Self-verification**: Checklist item: "Every unit of substantive work was performed by a specialist, not by me."
- **Reading is constrained**: The teamlead may read user requests, subagent results, and teamlead-memory.md, and sparingly a file the user explicitly references. No codebase browsing or "getting a feel" for the project themselves — that's delegated to `explore` or `code-analyst`.
- **Closing line**: "Keep your hands off the keyboard."
- **Conversational messages** (e.g., "what did you just do?") get a direct reply, not a specialist sub-task.
- **Ambiguity** should be resolved with the `question` tool before dispatching anything.

title: Divination handler: tool vs slash-command reply branching
content:
The `DivinationHandler` has two distinct reply paths sharing the same `_handleReading` pipeline via a `returnToolJson` flag:

1. **Slash-command path** (`returnToolJson=False`): Uses `DIVINATION_REPLY_TEMPLATE` (a `ChatSettingsKey`) with placeholders `{layoutName}`, `{drawnSymbolsBlock}`, `{interpretation}`. The drawn-symbols block is rendered by `BaseDivinationSystem.renderDrawnSymbolsBlock(reading, lang="ru")`. Image and text are sent as TWO separate `sendMessage` calls (photo-only first, then text-only) — not a combined caption.

2. **LLM-tool path** (`returnToolJson=True`): Returns a JSON dict with `layout`, `draws` (including `glyph` for runes), `interpretation` (bare LLM text, no template). Does NOT send a user-facing text message — the host LLM composes its own reply from the JSON. If an image was generated, sends it as a `MessageCategory.BOT` photo message with empty caption. Does NOT include `summary` or `imageGenerated` in the JSON (those were removed). Does NOT include image data in the JSON.

`_handleReading` signature: takes resolved `layout: Layout` object + `question: str` instead of raw `args: str`. Parsing + error-reply logic lives in caller helpers (`_handleReadingFromArgs` for slash commands, `_runReadingForTool` for LLM tools).

title: Gotcha: MessageSender.displayName does not exist — use .name
content:
The `EnsuredMessage.sender` object (`MessageSender`) has only `id`, `name`, `username` fields. There is NO `displayName` attribute. Using `.displayName` will silently `AttributeError` at runtime.

- Use `ensuredMessage.sender.name` for the user's full name (set from `user.full_name` in Telegram, `first_name + last_name` in Max)
- Use `ensuredMessage.sender.username` for the @-prefixed username

This was a common mistake in the divination handler plan and earlier code versions; the plan used `displayName` which doesn't exist in production.

title: Gotcha: LLMService.registerTool takes flat arguments, not nested LLMToolFunction
content:
`LLMService.registerTool()` signature is FLAT — it takes `name`, `description`, `parameters` (Sequence[LLMFunctionParameter]), `handler` directly. Do NOT wrap them in an `LLMToolFunction(...)` object.

Correct usage (matching `MediaHandler` and `WeatherHandler` patterns at `internal/bot/common/handlers/media.py:102-122` and `weather.py:138-160`):
```python
self.llmService.registerTool(
    name="do_tarot_reading",
    description="...",
    parameters=[
        LLMFunctionParameter(name="question", description="...", type=LLMParameterType.STRING, required=True),
    ],
    handler=self._llmToolDoTarotReading,
)
```

The old nested shape (`function=LLMToolFunction(...)`) appears in stale docs but doesn't match the actual signature.

title: Gotcha: MediaStatus and MessageType are enums, not strings
content:
When working with `media_attachments` repository or any media-related code, use the enum values — not string literals:

- `MediaStatus.DONE` (not `"processed"` or `"done"`) — members: `NEW`, `PENDING`, `DONE`, `FAILED` (defined in `internal/database/models.py`)
- `MessageType.IMAGE` (not `"photo"`) — defined in `internal/models/types.py`
- `MessageCategory.BOT_COMMAND_REPLY`, `MessageCategory.BOT_ERROR`, `MessageCategory.BOT` — StrEnum in `internal/database/models.py`

The plan used strings like `'photo'` and `'processed'` which would fail at runtime. The repo signatures take the enum type directly.

title: Gotcha: CommandCategory.AI doesn't exist — use CommandCategory.TOOLS
content:
The `CommandCategory` enum has members: `UNSPECIFIED`, `PRIVATE`, `ADMIN`, `TOOLS`, `SPAM`, `SPAM_ADMIN`, `TECHNICAL`. There is NO `AI` or `INFO` category.

The docs (`docs/llm/handlers.md`) are WRONG about these; always check the actual enum at `internal/bot/models/command_handlers.py:41-60`.

`CommandCategory.TOOLS` triggers the `ALLOW_TOOLS_COMMANDS` or `isBotOwner` gate. This means `/taro` and `/runes` are silently denied in chats where `allow-tools-commands=false` — consistent with `/draw` / `/analyze` / `/weather`.

title: Gotcha: Migration auto-discovery requires getMigration() factory function
content:
Migration auto-discovery at `internal/database/migrations/versions/__init__.py:87` checks `hasattr(module, "getMigration")` and SKIPS files without it. Every migration file MUST have a module-level `getMigration()` factory function returning a `Type[BaseMigration]` subclass.

Pattern (copy from migration_013):
```python
def getMigration() -> Type[BaseMigration]:
    return Migration014AddDivinationsTable
```

Without this, a migration file silently fails to load and the migration never runs.

title: Gotcha: Running pytest on internal/ subdirectories fails with ModuleNotFoundError
content:
You cannot run `./venv/bin/pytest internal/database/...` directly — collection fails with `ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'lib'` because pytest only adds the repo root to `sys.path` when targets are outside a package or when using configured `testpaths` from `pyproject.toml`.

Always use `make test` (which uses `testpaths = ["tests", "lib", "internal"]` from `pyproject.toml`) or run `pytest tests internal/database/...` (combining with a non-package top-level directory).

This affects all collocated tests under `internal/` (e.g., `internal/database/repositories/test_divinations.py`), not just new ones.

title: Guard against agent MM-state deliverable trap
content:
An agent can leave files in `git status MM` state — where the staged index (what `git commit` would use) differs from the working tree (what tests run against). This means tests can pass while the committed code contains a bug.

**The bug found:** The staged version of `divination.py` had an unconditional `if not returnToolJson: await self.sendMessage(...)` block AFTER the image-sending `else` branch, causing a second duplicate bare-text message on every slash-command with an image. The working tree had this fixed, so tests passed, but `git commit` without `git add` would ship the buggy version.

**Mitigation:**
1. Always check `git diff --cached` (or `git diff --staged`) before merging — this shows what would actually be committed
2. Add `sendMessage.call_count == 1` assertions in handler tests as regression guards
3. A verifier/QA agent should check `git status --short` for `MM` files and flag them

title: Guard: verify agent "fixed pre-existing bug" claims against git history
content:
An agent claimed to have "fixed a pre-existing test failure" where `drawsJson` was allegedly passed as a string instead of a list. The verifier disproved this by running `git show HEAD:internal/bot/common/handlers/divination.py` and `git diff HEAD` — the code at HEAD was already correct and unchanged.

**Rule for verifier agents:** When an implementer agent claims they "fixed a pre-existing bug" or "fixed a test failure," ALWAYS:
1. `git show HEAD:<file>` — view the code before the agent's session
2. `git diff HEAD <file>` — confirm what actually changed
3. If the change is zero (nothing changed), the claim is false or confused
4. Document the discrepancy in the verifier report

This is distinct from the `MM` state issue — this is about agents fabricating "heroic fixes" out of nothing.

title: Boundary test pattern: subprocess isolation for sys.modules checks
content:
Do NOT write boundary tests that check `sys.modules` membership live in the same pytest session. Prior tests in the same session can import `internal.services.llm` or `internal.bot` modules, polluting `sys.modules` and causing false positives/negatives.

Instead, use a **subprocess with a fresh Python interpreter**:
```python
import subprocess, sys

probeCode = (
    "import importlib, json, sys;"
    " importlib.import_module('lib.divination');"
    " leaked = sorted(m for m in sys.modules if m.startswith(('internal.services.llm', 'internal.bot')));"
    " print(json.dumps(leaked))"
)
result = subprocess.run([sys.executable, "-c", probeCode], capture_output=True, text=True, check=True)
assert result.stdout.strip() == "[]", f"Leaked: {result.stdout.strip()}"
```

This was used for the `lib/divination` boundary test (`testLibDivinationDoesNotPullBotSideModules`) to ensure the library stays decoupled from the bot runtime.

title: Divination reply template: DIVINATION_REPLY_TEMPLATE ChatSettingsKey
content:
A `ChatSettingsKey.DIVINATION_REPLY_TEMPLATE` was added for the structured slash-command reply (not used for LLM tool path). It has three placeholders:

- `{layoutName}` — the layout's Russian name (`layout.nameRu`)
- `{drawnSymbolsBlock}` — rendered by `BaseDivinationSystem.renderDrawnSymbolsBlock(reading, lang="ru")` showing a numbered list of drawn symbols with positions, Russian names, and optional ` (перевёрнута)` suffix for tarot reversals. Runes prepend their Unicode glyph (e.g., `ᚠ`).
- `{interpretation}` — the LLM-generated interpretation text

Default (shipped in `bot-defaults.toml`):
```
🎴 Расклад: {layoutName}

Выпавшие символы:
{drawnSymbolsBlock}

{interpretation}
```

Page: `ChatSettingsPage.BOT_OWNER_SYSTEM`. Type: `STRING`. The placeholder substitution uses `_safeFormat` from `lib/divination/base.py` (tolerates missing keys without error).

title: Symbol.glyph field: optional Unicode glyph for runes on Symbol dataclass
content:
The `Symbol` dataclass in `lib/divination/base.py` has an optional `glyph: Optional[str] = None` field. Inserted between `imagePromptFragment` and `metadata` in field order (satisfies `slots=True` defaults-after-non-defaults constraint).

- All 24 Elder Futhark runes in `lib/divination/decks/runes_elder_futhark.py` populated with their Unicode literals (U+16A0–U+16DF range, e.g. `ᚠ` for Fehu, `ᚢ` for Uruz)
- All 78 tarot cards leave `glyph=None` (no official Unicode Tarot block is used)
- `renderDrawnSymbolsBlock` prepends `"{glyph} "` when glyph is present
- LLM tool JSON `draws[]` entries include `"glyph"` key per symbol (string for runes, `null` for tarot)
- Tests verify: all 24 rune glyphs present, unique, single runic character; all 78 tarot None

title: Final divinations schema: 12 columns, no media_id or rng_seed
content:
The `divinations` table was edited in-place in migration_014 (not a separate migration 015, since `divination.enabled = false` and no production deployment existed). Final schema:

```
chat_id        INTEGER NOT NULL,
message_id     TEXT    NOT NULL,
user_id        INTEGER NOT NULL,
system_id      TEXT    NOT NULL,
deck_id        TEXT    NOT NULL,
layout_id      TEXT    NOT NULL,
question       TEXT    NOT NULL DEFAULT '',
draws_json     TEXT    NOT NULL,
interpretation TEXT    NOT NULL DEFAULT '',
image_prompt   TEXT,
invoked_via    TEXT    NOT NULL,
created_at     TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
PRIMARY KEY (chat_id, message_id)
```

Dropped columns:
- `media_id TEXT` (was linking to `media_attachments.file_unique_id` as soft FK) — removed because the normal media send pipeline handles image storage; no need for a separate FK
- `rng_seed INTEGER` — removed because it was never used

`draws_json` is passed as a `list[dict]` (not a JSON string) to the repository; `convertToSQLite` in `providers/utils.py:73-74` handles serialization automatically. The repo signature accepts `str | Sequence[dict]`.

title: Report doc convention: docs/reports/pr-YY.MM.DD-<short-name>.md
content:
Project postmortem/PR reports go in `docs/reports/` with filename pattern `pr-YY.MM.DD-<short-name>.md`. Convention from existing reports:

- Bilingual front-matter (English + Russian titles)
- **Author:** Vladimir Goshev
- **Date Created:** YYYY-MM-DD
- **Target Branch:** master
- **Source Branch:** feature/<name>
- **Brief Description** — what was delivered
- **Changed Files** — `git diff --stat HEAD~N` output
- **Table of Contents** for longer reports
- Structured in parts (A: What was built, B: Implementation journey, C: Insights, D: Limitations/Follow-ups, E: File inventory, F: Test count timeline)
- Bonus: Plan-vs-Reality matrix for features that had formal plans

Example: `pr-26.05.06-divination-handler.md` (572 lines) covers the full divination implementation journey.

title: random.SystemRandom() introduced as new lib/divination convention
content:
`lib/divination/drawing.py` uses `random.SystemRandom()` (cryptographically secure RNG from OS entropy) as the default RNG for drawing divination symbols. This is a **new convention** in the project — no prior usage existed.

The `drawSymbols` function has an injectable `rng: Optional[random.Random] = None` parameter for testability:
```python
def drawSymbols(deck, layout, *, supportsReversed, rng=None):
    rng = rng or random.SystemRandom()
    drawn = rng.sample(list(deck), k=layout.numSymbols)
    ...
```

Tests inject `random.Random(42)` (seeded `Random`, not `SystemRandom`) for deterministic assertions. This pattern is documented in `docs/llm/tasks.md` and the module docstring.

title: Divination feature: user preference notes
content:
Recorded user preferences from the divination feature development:

1. **Feature flag:** `divination.enabled = false` in `00-defaults`; user flips manually in `local/` config — team should not override
2. **Command aliases:** `/taro`, `/tarot`, `/таро` for tarot; `/runes`, `/rune`, `/руны` for runes
3. **Layout requirement:** Required on slash commands (missing → error + help); default `three_card`/`three_runes` applies ONLY to LLM tools when layout is omitted
4. **Translations:** Team writes Russian translations using standard terminology; no user review needed
5. **Prompts:** Implementer's draft or improved version is fine; no user review needed
6. **Golden tests:** Write collector + scenarios + test skeleton; user records fixtures and reviews
7. **Reply format:** Structured template with emoji (🎴 for tarot, 🪨 for runes), Russian-only names, rune Unicode glyphs prepended
8. **Content across sessions:** Long, multi-phase collaborative development via agent delegation with architect → parallel implementers → verifier → docs updater workflow

title: Gotcha: Migration up/down are async, take BaseSQLProvider — docs are stale
content:
The migration API in the actual codebase is `async def up(self, sqlProvider: BaseSQLProvider) -> None` and `async def down(self, sqlProvider: BaseSQLProvider) -> None`. Migrations must return a `Type[BaseMigration]` subclass via a module-level `getMigration()` factory function.

The docs at `docs/llm/database.md:198-227` show a STALE sync `cursor`-based API — do NOT follow that. Always check `internal/database/migrations/versions/migration_013_remove_timestamp_defaults.py` as the canonical example. The real pattern is:

```python
class Migration014AddDivinationsTable(BaseMigration):
    version: int = 14
    description: str = "Add divinations table"
    async def up(self, sqlProvider: BaseSQLProvider) -> None:
        await sqlProvider.batchExecute([ParametrizedQuery("CREATE TABLE ...")])
    async def down(self, sqlProvider: BaseSQLProvider) -> None:
        await sqlProvider.batchExecute([ParametrizedQuery("DROP TABLE ...")])

def getMigration() -> Type[BaseMigration]:
    return Migration014AddDivinationsTable
```

Key differences from the stale docs:
- `async def up(self, sqlProvider)` — NOT `def up(self, cursor)`
- Takes `BaseSQLProvider`, not a raw `sqlite3.Cursor`
- Uses `ParametrizedQuery` wrappers and `sqlProvider.batchExecute()`

title: Gotcha: Repository ctor takes DatabaseManager, not Database
content:
All repositories in `internal/database/repositories/` take `manager: DatabaseManager` in `__init__`, not `db: 'Database'` as the docs at `docs/llm/database.md:386` claim.

Correct pattern (copy from `internal/database/repositories/media_attachments.py:56-62`):
```python
class MediaAttachmentsRepository(BaseRepository):
    __slots__ = ()
    def __init__(self, manager: DatabaseManager) -> None:
        super().__init__(manager)
```

Then wired in `Database.__init__` as `self.mediaAttachments = MediaAttachmentsRepository(self.manager)` (not `Database(self)`).

The repo accesses the provider via `await self.manager.getProvider(chatId=chatId, readonly=False)`, not via a stored `Database` reference.

title: Gotcha: RateLimiterManager.applyLimit is NOT idempotent — each call consumes a slot
content:
`RateLimiterManager.applyLimit(queue, key)` in `lib/rate_limiter/manager.py:283` records every call as a new request timestamp in the sliding window — it is NOT idempotent in any time window.

This means the existing WeatherHandler pattern of calling `await self.llmService.rateLimit(chatId, chatSettings)` BEFORE `generateText`/`generateImage` (which also call `rateLimit` internally) consumes 2-3 slots per command (rateLimit + generateText + optionally generateImage). This is acceptable and matches existing handler patterns, but it's a design limitation that can starve low-limit chats faster.

Do NOT try to fix this by skipping the inner rateLimit — that would require changing LLMService internals. Instead, document the behavior and accept the extra slot consumption.

title: Convention: Timestamps must use dbUtils.getCurrentTimestamp() at insert time
content:
Since migration 013 removed `DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP` from all tables, application code must set `created_at` / `updated_at` timestamps explicitly. Use `dbUtils.getCurrentTimestamp()` from `internal/database/utils.py` which returns a portable timestamp value.

Insert pattern:
```python
from .. import utils as dbUtils
# ...
"createdAt": dbUtils.getCurrentTimestamp(),
```

This is already documented in AGENTS.md and `docs/llm/database.md §7` but implementers frequently forget. Every new table MUST set timestamps in the INSERT statement — no relying on DB defaults.

title: Gotcha: Composite PK (chat_id, message_id) — second insert for same message fails silently
content:
When using a composite natural key PK like `(chat_id, message_id)` for the `divinations` table (or any table), inserting a second row with the same `chat_id` + `message_id` will raise a UNIQUE constraint violation — there's no `ON CONFLICT` handling.

This is a real risk when the same handler pipeline is invoked twice for the same message (e.g. LLM tool retries, or race between command handler and LLM tool path).

Mitigation pattern: wrap the INSERT in try/except, log the error, and proceed with the user-facing reply regardless. The user-facing reply must succeed even if DB insert fails:
```python
try:
    await self.db.divinations.insertReading(...)
except Exception as e:
    logger.error(f"Failed to insert divination: {e}")
```

Do NOT wrap the entire handler body — only the insert. The reply is sent before or separately from the DB write.

title: Docs/llm/database.md has stale migration and repository constructor patterns
content:
Two sections in `docs/llm/database.md` are stale and disagree with the source code:

1. **Migration API (§5, around line 198-227):** Shows sync `cursor`-based API (`def up(self, cursor): cursor.execute(...)`). The actual API is `async def up(self, sqlProvider: BaseSQLProvider) -> None` using `ParametrizedQuery` and `batchExecute()`. See `internal/database/migrations/versions/migration_013_remove_timestamp_defaults.py` for the real pattern.

2. **Repository constructor (§5, around line 386):** Shows `def __init__(self, db: 'Database')`. The actual pattern is `def __init__(self, manager: DatabaseManager)` (confirmed in `media_attachments.py:56-62`, `chat_messages.py`).

These need updating to match the codebase. Project convention: code is truth; follow the source, not the stale docs.

title: Divination config split: prompts in bot-defaults.toml, feature flags in divination.toml
content:
The divination feature defaults are split across two TOML files:

1. **Prompt defaults** (4 keys: `taro-system-prompt`, `runes-system-prompt`, `divination-user-prompt-template`, `divination-image-prompt-template`) — append to `configs/00-defaults/bot-defaults.toml` under `[bot.defaults]`. These are ChatSettingsKey defaults, loaded via the existing `[bot.defaults] → ChatSettingsDict` pipeline.

2. **Feature flag config** (`enabled`, `tarot-enabled`, `runes-enabled`, `image-generation`, `tools-enabled`) — create a new `configs/00-defaults/divination.toml` under `[divination]` section. Mirrors the `resender.toml` pattern. The handler reads these via `self.configManager.get("divination", {}).get("enabled", False)`.

This split exists because `[bot.defaults]` keys are namespace-filtered to only include values that match `ChatSettingsKey` enum members, while the feature flags are handled by `ConfigManager.get()` directly — they're not chat-settings.

title: MessageSender.displayName AttributeError trap — use .name instead
content: The `MessageSender` class (internal/models/types.py) has a `.name` attribute, NOT `.displayName`. The divination handler plan used `MessageSender.displayName` which would raise AttributeError at runtime. Always use `.name` to get the sender's display name. This is a gotcha that can mislead developers familiar with other platforms' naming conventions.

title: RateLimiter.applyLimit is not idempotent — each call consumes a slot
content: The `RateLimiter.applyLimit()` method always consumes a rate limit slot on each invocation. It is NOT idempotent — calling it multiple times for the same operation will exhaust the rate limit window prematurely. The divination handler plan incorrectly assumed `applyLimit` was safe to call proactively without consumption. This behavior means code should call `applyLimit` exactly once before the operation it guards, not speculatively.

title: LLM tool registration uses flat dict form, not nested class structures
content: The `LLMService.registerTool()` API takes tool definitions as flat dicts with keys like `name`, `description`, and `parameters`, NOT nested Python class structures. The divination handler plan initially used a nested `LLMToolFunction` class approach which didn't match the real API. The parameters dict uses JSON Schema format (type, properties, required). Check `LLMService.registerTool()` signature in `internal/services/llm/llm.py` for the exact shape.
